structure.hours: 12 hours of night, each with challenges

realmDetails.description: The Egyptian underworld through which the sun god Ra travels nightly and souls of the dead must navigate

name: Egyptian Underworld Journey
connections: Nile River - above-world counterpart (mirrors Duat structure)
Nun - primordial waters surrounding the journey
Fields of Aaru - final destination for blessed
Lake of Fire - place of judgment and purification
description:
The Duat is the Egyptian underworld, a realm of darkness, danger, and transformation that must be traversed
                each night by Ra in his solar barque and by the souls of the deceased seeking eternal life. The journey through
                the Duat's twelve hours represents the sun's passage from west (death/sunset) to east (rebirth/dawn), mirroring
                the soul's journey from death to resurrection.

richMetadata.structure: The Duat is organized as a vertical journey through 12 hours of night, each hour presenting distinct challenges, gates, and landscapes. Ra's solar barque travels eastward through four main divisions: entry halls, water realms, dangerous passages, and transformation chambers.
connections: Nile River - above-world counterpart (mirrors Duat structure)
Nun - primordial waters surrounding the journey
Fields of Aaru - final destination for blessed
Lake of Fire - place of judgment and purification
richMetadata.significance: The Duat represents the cyclical regeneration of life through death. It embodies the principle of transformation and daily resurrection, essential to Egyptian beliefs about immortality and the eternal return. The journey ensures cosmic order (Ma'at) is maintained.
parallels: Sumerian descent of Inanna - underworld journey
Greek Underworld - realm of the dead
Mesopotamian Irkalla - land of no return
sources: Book of the Dead (Pert em Hru)

keyMyths.title: Ra's Nightly Journey
keyMyths.description: Each night, Ra, the sun god, travels through the Duat in his solar barque, battling the serpent Apophis and other monstrous entities. His successful journey ensures the sun's rebirth and the continuation of cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart
keyMyths.description: Upon entering the Duat, the deceased's heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart is lighter, the soul is worthy of eternal life; if heavier, it's devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls.

keyMyths.title: Osiris's Role in the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Osiris, the god of the afterlife, presides over the Duat and judges the deceased. He offers guidance and protection to those deemed worthy, granting them access to the Field of Reeds.

keyMyths.title: The Twelve Hours of the Night
keyMyths.description: The Duat is divided into twelve regions, each representing an hour of the night, filled with unique trials, demons, and deities. The deceased must navigate these challenges to achieve immortality, mirroring Ra's journey.

keyMyths.title: The Resurrection of Osiris
keyMyths.description: The myth of Osiris's murder, dismemberment, and resurrection by Isis is central to the Duat's significance. It symbolizes the possibility of overcoming death and achieving eternal life through divine intervention and ritual.

sources.name: The Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells and incantations intended to guide the deceased through the Duat and ensure their successful passage to the afterlife. Papyrus scrolls containing these spells were often buried with the dead.
sources.title: The Book of the Dead

sources.name: The Book of Amduat
sources.description: An important funerary text describing Ra's nightly journey through the twelve divisions of the Duat, detailing the deities and creatures he encounters and the challenges he overcomes.
sources.title: The Book of Amduat

sources.name: The Book of Gates
sources.description: Another funerary text focusing on the twelve gates that Ra must pass through during his nightly journey, emphasizing the obstacles and transformations involved in the underworld passage.
sources.title: The Book of Gates

sources.name: Pyramid Texts
sources.description: The oldest known religious texts from ancient Egypt, inscribed on the walls of pyramids, offering insights into early beliefs about the afterlife and the role of the pharaoh in the Duat.
sources.title: Pyramid Texts

sources.name: Plutarch, Isis and Osiris
sources.description: A Greek writer's account of the Osiris myth, providing a valuable, though sometimes Hellenized, perspective on Egyptian religious beliefs and practices concerning death and resurrection.
sources.title: Plutarch, Isis and Osiris
